lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: Good morning, coffee lovers! Welcome to The Daily Grind, your daily dose of specialty coffee news. I'm Milo.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: And I'm Riley. If your cup is empty, now's the time to fix that. Because today we're talking about money, climate, and some very expensive Italian machines.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: It's Saturday, May 2nd. And the biggest story in coffee right now is prices. Coffee is at a 50-year high.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: Fifty years. That's wild. My parents weren't even drinking specialty coffee back then. They were still on instant.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: So what's driving this? Climate change is the big one. Brazil and Vietnam, the two largest producers, have both been hit hard by drought and extreme weather. When supply drops and demand stays steady, prices go to the moon.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: And it's not just weather. There's speculation too. Commodity traders see coffee as the next big thing to bet on, which drives futures prices even higher. It's a perfect storm.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: The real tragedy is who bears the cost. Smallholder farmers often don't see much of that price increase. The commodity markets capture most of the gains, while the people actually growing the coffee are struggling with the same climate disasters.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: It's the classic middleman problem. The farmer gets squeezed, the consumer pays more, and somewhere in between a commodities trader buys a second yacht.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: Direct trade models help, but they only reach a tiny fraction of the global supply. For most coffee, the C-market price is what determines everything, and that market is brutal.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: Which brings us to Colombia. Colombian coffee farmers are increasingly switching to cacao as the climate warms and coffee prices become volatile.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: Cacao makes sense for a few reasons. It tolerates heat better, it grows in similar elevations, and the chocolate market is booming. If you're a farmer watching your coffee yields drop year after year, cacao starts looking very attractive.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: But Colombia is synonymous with coffee. The Juan Valdez brand, the national identity, it's all wrapped up in those Arabica beans. If farmers walk away, what happens to Colombian coffee culture?

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: It's a real identity crisis. And it's happening in other origins too. Kenya, Ethiopia, Guatemala, they're all facing similar pressures. Coffee is a finicky crop. It needs specific temperatures, specific rainfall, specific altitudes. Climate change doesn't care about your heritage.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: I read that some Ethiopian farmers are moving their plantations uphill, chasing cooler temperatures. But there's only so much mountain left.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: Yeah, altitude is finite. You can't just keep climbing forever. At some point you run out of viable land, and then what?

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: On a lighter note, the specialty coffee community is building some really cool tools to help enthusiasts navigate this chaotic market. Have you seen RoastDB?

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: The searchable database with over 3,800 specialty beans? Yeah, I spent an hour on it last night instead of sleeping.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: It's so well done. You can filter by origin, process, roaster, flavor notes. And it's crowdsourced, so it keeps growing. I found three Ethiopian naturals I've never heard of just by browsing.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: Discovery is the hardest part of being a coffee enthusiast. There are thousands of roasters worldwide, and most of them have zero marketing budget. Tools like RoastDB surface the hidden gems.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: And there's another one called Brewmind. The founder built it because they kept wasting good beans on bad recipes.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: I feel that in my soul. How many bags of expensive single-origin have I ruined because I couldn't dial in the grind?

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: Brewmind helps you track your brews, rate them, and iterate on your parameters. It's like a lab notebook for your morning ritual. Over time, you figure out exactly how to extract the best from each bean.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: These tools are doing for coffee what Untappd did for craft beer. Making it social, data-driven, and way more fun to explore.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: I love that both RoastDB and Brewmind were built by enthusiasts who were frustrated with existing options. That's always where the best tools come from.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: Necessity is the mother of invention, and bad espresso is the mother of coffee apps.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: One more thing before we go. Used La Marzocco machines are now being coveted by cafe owners and collectors.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: La Marzocco? The Ferrari of espresso machines? People are collecting them now?

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: Apparently. A well-maintained Linea Classic from the nineties can sell for more than it cost new. There's something about those older Italian builds, the dual boilers, the saturated groups, that people just trust more than newer models.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: It's like vintage synthesizers or mechanical watches. There's a romance to analog craftsmanship that digital interfaces can't replicate.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: And they're built to last. A La Marzocco from 1995, if you service it properly, will probably outlive most of the smart espresso machines being released today.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: Those smart machines with their apps and touchscreens are cool, but when the manufacturer stops updating the software, you've got a very expensive brick. A mechanical lever and a boiler? That'll work forever.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: My favorite detail is that some cafe owners are specifically seeking out machines with the old paddle groups instead of the modern buttons. They say the pre-infusion is more intuitive.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: Paddle groups are gorgeous. It's like shifting a manual transmission. There's a tactile satisfaction that buttons just can't match.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: So the lesson is: buy once, cry once. Get the machine that doesn't need firmware updates to make espresso.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: Exactly. Let's wrap this up. Today we covered coffee prices hitting a 50-year high, Colombian farmers moving to cacao, discovery tools like RoastDB and Brewmind, and the cult of vintage La Marzocco.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: It's a complicated time to love coffee. The industry is under serious pressure from climate change, but the community is responding with better tools, more transparency, and a deep appreciation for craft.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: And a word of advice: maybe stock up on your favorite beans now, before prices climb even higher. Your future self will thank you.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: Just don't hoard so much that they go stale. There's a fine line between smart stocking and ending up with a freezer full of oxidized Yirgacheffe.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: The key is vacuum sealing and a deep freezer. Treat your beans like fine wine, not like leftovers.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: Words to live by.

lines.speaker: Milo
lines.text: Thanks for joining us on The Daily Grind. I'm Milo.

lines.speaker: Riley
lines.text: And I'm Riley. Brew something beautiful today, and we'll catch you tomorrow for more coffee news.
